content:
4 min read
Just now

--

The “flawless” look is out. Here’s the editing style replacing it — and how to get it right.

For the last decade, "good editing" meant smoothing everything out — poreless skin, blown-out highlights pulled back to perfection, that slightly plastic HDR glow. It photographed well on a thumbnail. It just didn't look like a real person anymore.
That era is ending.
Search data backs this up: on stock platforms like Envato, searches for “unfiltered” images are climbing fast, and photographers surveyed heading into 2026 keep circling back to the same word — authenticity. Wedding, portrait, and lifestyle photographers are being asked for images that feel like a moment, not a magazine cover. Retouchers are being asked to edit less, not more.
If you’re a photographer, content creator, or someone who hires editing work, here’s what’s actually changing — and how to adjust your workflow so your photos don’t look dated in six months.

1. Skin should look like skin
The single biggest shift is in retouching. The old standard was “remove everything” — blemishes, texture, pores, fine lines. The new standard is “remove distractions, keep the person.”
What this looks like in practice:
— Spot-heal temporary blemishes (a pimple, a scratch) — leave permanent texture (freckles, laugh lines, pores) alone
— Use frequency separation at a much lighter hand than you’re used to, or skip it entirely for casual shots
— Even out blotchy tones instead of erasing texture — the goal is “well-rested,” not “airbrushed”
If a client can’t tell the photo was edited at all, you did it right.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

2. Color grading is warm, soft, and a little imperfect
Punchy, ultra-saturated, high-contrast looks are giving way to muted, film-inspired tones — think soft shadows, warm midtones, slightly lifted blacks. It’s the difference between a photo that screams “edited” and one that just feels nice to look at.
Quick recipe to try in Lightroom or Photoshop:
— Drop highlights slightly, lift shadows just a touch (reduces harsh contrast)
— Add a subtle warm tint to midtones (+5 to +10 on the yellow/orange side)
— Reduce global saturation by 5–10%, then selectively boost skin tones back up
— Add a very light grain — it breaks up the “too clean” digital look
3. Imperfection is a feature, not a mistake
This is the biggest mindset shift for editors. Motion blur from a kid running, a stray hair, uneven lighting from a window — these used to get “fixed.” Now they’re often the whole reason the photo works. The goal isn’t to erase reality, it’s to make it feel intentional.
Ask yourself before every edit: does removing this make the photo more true, or just more sterile?

4. AI tools are for speed, not for the final call
AI-assisted culling and editing tools (Aftershoot, Lightroom’s AI masking, etc.) are everywhere in 2026 — and that’s not a contradiction to the “authentic” trend, it’s actually what’s enabling it. Editors are using AI to handle the repetitive 80% of the work (culling, basic color matching, background cleanup) so they have more time to make thoughtful, human calls on the remaining 20% — the part that actually decides whether a photo feels real or fake.
The photographers getting the best results in 2026 aren’t choosing between AI and human judgment. They’re using AI to buy back time for the human part.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

5. Consistency still matters — just not perfection
One thing hasn’t changed: brands and creators still need a cohesive look across their feed or portfolio. The trend isn’t “stop editing,” it’s “edit toward realism, consistently.” A consistent, natural preset applied thoughtfully across a shoot still reads as professional — it just doesn’t scream “filter.”
The takeaway
If you’re editing photos in 2026, the winning formula is simple: enhance, don’t erase. Fix what’s temporary, keep what’s true, warm up the tones, and let a little imperfection stay in the frame. It’s a harder skill than heavy retouching, honestly — restraint always is. But it’s what people actually want to see right now.
